forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+---
+assignees:
+- claude-code
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: '8880'
+title: Make notify file-watcher teardown non-blocking (FileWatcher + EntityWatcher) to fix FSEvents-contention timeouts
+---
+## What
+
+**Root cause (profiled 2026-05-21)** of the remaining in-process-server / watcher test timeouts under full-workspace `cargo nextest run`: dropping a `notify` file-watcher blocks for ~5s on macOS FSEvents stream teardown, and that teardown serializes through the OS FSEvents subsystem — so under load (~10+ watchers torn down concurrently) it queues and crosses the 300s nextest kill. This is the SAME OS-subsystem-serialization class as the already-fixed `configd` proxy issue. It is the dominant remaining cost after the proxy + tokio-runtime fixes (commit `62f5d065a`): each in-process MCP server's `shutdown()` spends ~5057ms in `stop_file_watching()` dropping the `AsyncDebouncer`.
+
+Two watchers share the pattern:
+- `crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/file_watcher.rs` — `FileWatcher` holds `debouncer: Option<AsyncDebouncer<RecommendedWatcher>>`. `stop_watching()` (called by `stop_file_watching()` and by `Drop`) does `self.debouncer.take()` and drops it synchronously on the caller's thread.
+- `crates/swissarmyhammer-entity/src/watcher.rs` — `EntityWatcher` holds `_watcher: RecommendedWatcher`; its `Drop` sends a shutdown oneshot but the `_watcher` field then drops synchronously, blocking on FSEvents teardown.
+
+## Fix
+
+Detach the blocking notify-watcher drop to a background OS thread so the caller's shutdown/Drop returns immediately while the OS teardown completes off the critical path. This preserves the hot-reload / entity-watch feature entirely — only the teardown timing moves off-path.
+
+- `FileWatcher::stop_watching` (`crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/file_watcher.rs:203`): when taking the debouncer, move it into `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(debouncer))` instead of dropping inline. `std::thread::spawn` (not `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`) because `Drop`/`stop_watching` can run outside a tokio runtime. Keep the existing `event_handle.abort()` and `event_rx` cleanup.
+- `EntityWatcher::drop` (`crates/swissarmyhammer-entity/src/watcher.rs:121`): send the shutdown signal as today, then move `_watcher` out (make it `Option<RecommendedWatcher>`) and `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(watcher))` so the FSEvents teardown does not block the dropping thread.
+- Add a short doc comment at each site explaining WHY (FSEvents teardown blocks ~5s and serializes across processes under load; detaching keeps shutdown fast without changing watch behavior).
+
+### Caveats to handle
+- The watched root is often a `tempfile::TempDir` that may be deleted right after the test drops the watcher. A detached teardown watching an already-deleted path is benign for FSEvents but may log; ensure no panic and no test-visible error.
+- Bound the blast radius: detached drops run on short-lived threads; concurrency is bounded by nextest parallelism (~32), so at most ~32 transient teardown threads — acceptable. Do NOT introduce an unbounded thread spawn in a tight loop.
+
+## Acceptance Criteria
+- [ ] `FileWatcher::stop_watching` and `EntityWatcher::drop` no longer block the caller on the notify-watcher teardown; the watcher object is dropped on a detached thread.
+- [ ] Measured: an in-process MCP server `shutdown()` drops from ~5s to milliseconds (record before/after for one server).
+- [ ] The previously-flaky tests pass FAST under full-workspace load (not just isolation): the in-process MCP-server cluster (`test_mcp_server_*`, `test_client_list_tools`/`test_client_call_tool`, kanban-app `state::tests` open-board), `mcp::file_watcher::tests::test_file_watcher_start_watching_replaces_previous`, and `swissarmyhammer-entity watcher::tests` (`entity_watcher_drop_sends_shutdown`, `test_attachment_create_emits_event`, `test_attachment_remove_emits_event`). If the event-delivery tests (attachment create/remove) remain slow because of FSEvents *event delivery* latency (a different facet than teardown), document that and file a follow-up rather than forcing it here.
+- [ ] No change to observable watch behavior: prompt hot-reload and entity-cache event emission still work (existing event tests pass in isolation).
+- [ ] `cargo build --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check` clean.
+- [ ] No serial guards or nextest `slow-timeout` overrides reintroduced.
+
+## Tests
+- [ ] Unit/behavior test that `FileWatcher::stop_watching` returns promptly (e.g. completes in well under 1s even after watching a real dir) — guards against a regression that re-blocks teardown.
+- [ ] Equivalent promptness guard for `EntityWatcher` drop.
+- [ ] Existing watcher event tests still pass in isolation (no behavior regression).
+- [ ] Full-workspace reliability: run `cargo nextest run --workspace` up to 3 consecutive times (early-bail on any cluster timeout), confirming the FSEvents cluster passes fast. Record honest timings.
+
+## Workflow
+- Use `/tdd` — add the teardown-promptness guard test(s) first, then detach the drops. Measure before/after server shutdown. Verify with consecutive full-workspace runs. #test-failure
\ No newline at end of file

-title: Make notify file-watcher teardown non-blocking (FileWatcher + EntityWatcher) to fix FSEvents-contention timeouts
----
-## What
-
-**Root cause (profiled 2026-05-21)** of the remaining in-process-server / watcher test timeouts under full-workspace `cargo nextest run`: dropping a `notify` file-watcher blocks for ~5s on macOS FSEvents stream teardown, and that teardown serializes through the OS FSEvents subsystem — so under load (~10+ watchers torn down concurrently) it queues and crosses the 300s nextest kill. This is the SAME OS-subsystem-serialization class as the already-fixed `configd` proxy issue. It is the dominant remaining cost after the proxy + tokio-runtime fixes (commit `62f5d065a`): each in-process MCP server's `shutdown()` spends ~5057ms in `stop_file_watching()` dropping the `AsyncDebouncer`.
-
-Two watchers share the pattern:
-- `crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/file_watcher.rs` — `FileWatcher` holds `debouncer: Option<AsyncDebouncer<RecommendedWatcher>>`. `stop_watching()` (called by `stop_file_watching()` and by `Drop`) does `self.debouncer.take()` and drops it synchronously on the caller's thread.
-- `crates/swissarmyhammer-entity/src/watcher.rs` — `EntityWatcher` holds `_watcher: RecommendedWatcher`; its `Drop` sends a shutdown oneshot but the `_watcher` field then drops synchronously, blocking on FSEvents teardown.
-
-## Fix
-
-Detach the blocking notify-watcher drop to a background OS thread so the caller's shutdown/Drop returns immediately while the OS teardown completes off the critical path. This preserves the hot-reload / entity-watch feature entirely — only the teardown timing moves off-path.
-
-- `FileWatcher::stop_watching` (`crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/file_watcher.rs:203`): when taking the debouncer, move it into `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(debouncer))` instead of dropping inline. `std::thread::spawn` (not `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`) because `Drop`/`stop_watching` can run outside a tokio runtime. Keep the existing `event_handle.abort()` and `event_rx` cleanup.
-- `EntityWatcher::drop` (`crates/swissarmyhammer-entity/src/watcher.rs:121`): send the shutdown signal as today, then move `_watcher` out (make it `Option<RecommendedWatcher>`) and `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(watcher))` so the FSEvents teardown does not block the dropping thread.
-- Add a short doc comment at each site explaining WHY (FSEvents teardown blocks ~5s and serializes across processes under load; detaching keeps shutdown fast without changing watch behavior).
-
-### Caveats to handle
-- The watched root is often a `tempfile::TempDir` that may be deleted right after the test drops the watcher. A detached teardown watching an already-deleted path is benign for FSEvents but may log; ensure no panic and no test-visible error.
-- Bound the blast radius: detached drops run on short-lived threads; concurrency is bounded by nextest parallelism (~32), so at most ~32 transient teardown threads — acceptable. Do NOT introduce an unbounded thread spawn in a tight loop.
-
-## Acceptance Criteria
-- [ ] `FileWatcher::stop_watching` and `EntityWatcher::drop` no longer block the caller on the notify-watcher teardown; the watcher object is dropped on a detached thread.
-- [ ] Measured: an in-process MCP server `shutdown()` drops from ~5s to milliseconds (record before/after for one server).
-- [ ] The previously-flaky tests pass FAST under full-workspace load (not just isolation): the in-process MCP-server cluster (`test_mcp_server_*`, `test_client_list_tools`/`test_client_call_tool`, kanban-app `state::tests` open-board), `mcp::file_watcher::tests::test_file_watcher_start_watching_replaces_previous`, and `swissarmyhammer-entity watcher::tests` (`entity_watcher_drop_sends_shutdown`, `test_attachment_create_emits_event`, `test_attachment_remove_emits_event`). If the event-delivery tests (attachment create/remove) remain slow because of FSEvents *event delivery* latency (a different facet than teardown), document that and file a follow-up rather than forcing it here.
-- [ ] No change to observable watch behavior: prompt hot-reload and entity-cache event emission still work (existing event tests pass in isolation).
-- [ ] `cargo build --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check` clean.
-- [ ] No serial guards or nextest `slow-timeout` overrides reintroduced.
-
-## Tests
-- [ ] Unit/behavior test that `FileWatcher::stop_watching` returns promptly (e.g. completes in well under 1s even after watching a real dir) — guards against a regression that re-blocks teardown.
-- [ ] Equivalent promptness guard for `EntityWatcher` drop.
-- [ ] Existing watcher event tests still pass in isolation (no behavior regression).
-- [ ] Full-workspace reliability: run `cargo nextest run --workspace` up to 3 consecutive times (early-bail on any cluster timeout), confirming the FSEvents cluster passes fast. Record honest timings.
-
-## Workflow
-- Use `/tdd` — add the teardown-promptness guard test(s) first, then detach the drops. Measure before/after server shutdown. Verify with consecutive full-workspace runs. #test-failure
\ No newline at end of file

If the event-delivery tests (attachment create/remove) remain slow because of FSEvents *event delivery* latency (a different facet than teardown), document that and file a follow-up rather than forcing it here. -- NOT MET: cluster still slow (18-45s each); see findings.

\ No newline at end of file
+- Use `/tdd` — add the teardown-promptness guard test(s) first, then detach the drops. Measure before/after server shutdown. Verify with consecutive full-workspace runs. #test-failure
+
+---
+
+## IMPLEMENTATION FINDINGS (2026-05-21, claude-code) — STOPPED FOR GUIDANCE
+
+The teardown detach was implemented exactly as specified and is verified correct, BUT it does NOT fix the in-process server shutdown slowness. Profiling found a SECOND, distinct cause.
+
+### Profiling data (single `test_client_list_tools`, after the teardown fix)
+```
+TIMING start_mcp_server   = 22.5 ms
+TIMING   lock acquire     = 4.95 s   <-- the real cost
+TIMING   stop_watching    = 60 us    <-- teardown detach works perfectly
+TIMING stop_file_watching = 4.95 s
+TIMING server.shutdown    = 4.95 s
+```
+
+### Diagnosis
+- The teardown detach is correct: `FileWatcher::stop_watching` now drops the `AsyncDebouncer` in 60us (was the suspected ~5s). Detach works.
+- The actual ~5s cost in `shutdown()` is the `self.file_watcher.lock().await` in `McpServer::stop_file_watching` (server.rs:1121). It waits ~4.95s for the tokio Mutex.
+- The lock is held by the BACKGROUND `start_file_watching` task (spawned by `spawn_background_file_watcher`), which holds `file_watcher.lock()` across the entire `FileWatcher::start_watching()` — and on macOS the slow op there is registering the FSEvents stream: `debouncer.watcher().watch(path, RecursiveMode::Recursive)` (NOT the drop). So shutdown blocks behind startup finishing its watch registration, then tears it down.
+- This is a different FSEvents facet (stream CREATION/REGISTRATION under a held lock) than the task's stated root cause (stream TEARDOWN). The task explicitly anticipated this kind of split: "If the teardown fix does not make the cluster reliably pass under load, STOP and report with data rather than re-adding workarounds."
+
+### What is DONE and verified
+- Teardown detach in both sites (`FileWatcher::stop_watching`, `EntityWatcher::Drop`) with WHY doc comments. `std::thread::spawn`, not spawn_blocking.
+- `EntityWatcher._watcher` -> `Option<RecommendedWatcher>` so Drop can move it out.
+- Two promptness guard tests added (TDD: EntityWatcher test went RED at 1.16s pre-fix, GREEN post-fix; FileWatcher drop now 60us).
+- All `swissarmyhammer-entity watcher::tests` and `swissarmyhammer-tools mcp::file_watcher::tests` pass (102 tests). In-process server cluster tests PASS but are still SLOW (~5s shutdown each).
+
+### Decision needed (would extend beyond the assigned plan)
+Options to actually make shutdown fast (pick one before I proceed):
+1. Make `start_file_watching` register the FSEvents watch WITHOUT holding `file_watcher.lock()` across the slow `.watch()` call (e.g. build+watch the debouncer first, then briefly lock only to store it; on shutdown, if startup is mid-flight, signal it to abort and skip storing). 
+2. On shutdown, `try_lock()` the watcher and, if the startup task still holds it, abort that background task (via its JoinHandle) instead of awaiting the lock — let the half-built debouncer drop on its own detached thread.
+3. Detach the FSEvents `.watch()` registration off the lock-held critical path during startup.
+
+I have NOT implemented any of these — they go beyond the assigned teardown-detach plan. Awaiting guidance.
\ No newline at end of file

 Detach the blocking notify-watcher drop to a background OS thread so the caller's shutdown/Drop returns immediately while the OS teardown completes off the critical path. This preserves the hot-reload / entity-watch feature entirely — only the teardown timing moves off-path.

-- `FileWatcher::stop_watching` (`crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/file_watcher.rs:203`): when taking the debouncer, move it into `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(debouncer))` instead of dropping inline. `std::thread::spawn` (not `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`) because `Drop`/`stop_watching` can run outside a tokio runtime. Keep the existing `event_handle.abort()` and `event_rx` cleanup.
-- `EntityWatcher::drop` (`crates/swissarmyhammer-entity/src/watcher.rs:121`): send the shutdown signal as today, then move `_watcher` out (make it `Option<RecommendedWatcher>`) and `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(watcher))` so the FSEvents teardown does not block the dropping thread.
-- Add a short doc comment at each site explaining WHY (FSEvents teardown blocks ~5s and serializes across processes under load; detaching keeps shutdown fast without changing watch behavior).
+- `FileWatcher::stop_watching`: when taking the debouncer, move it into `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(debouncer))` instead of dropping inline. DONE.
+- `EntityWatcher::drop`: send shutdown signal, then move `_watcher` (now `Option<RecommendedWatcher>`) into `std::thread::spawn(move || drop(watcher))`. DONE.

-### Caveats to handle
-- The watched root is often a `tempfile::TempDir` that may be deleted right after the test drops the watcher. A detached teardown watching an already-deleted path is benign for FSEvents but may log; ensure no panic and no test-visible error.
-- Bound the blast radius: detached drops run on short-lived threads; concurrency is bounded by nextest parallelism (~32), so at most ~32 transient teardown threads — acceptable. Do NOT introduce an unbounded thread spawn in a tight loop.
+### Second facet (lock contention) — FIXED 2026-05-21 (option 1 + non-blocking shutdown)
+Teardown detach alone did NOT make `shutdown()` fast: profiling showed `shutdown()` still ~5s, spent waiting on `self.file_watcher.lock().await` because the BACKGROUND `start_file_watching` task held that lock across the slow FSEvents `.watch()` registration. Fixed per approved direction:
+- `FileWatcher::start(callback)` — new associated constructor that builds the debouncer + performs the slow `.watch()` registration WITHOUT `&mut self` / without any shared lock, returning a started `FileWatcher`. `start_watching` is now a thin `&mut self` wrapper over it.
+- `McpServer::start_file_watching_with_callback` builds via `FileWatcher::start` OFF the `file_watcher` lock, then acquires the lock only briefly to store the result.
+- `McpServer` gains `file_watcher_task` (JoinHandle of in-flight startup, aborted on shutdown) and `file_watch_stopped` (AtomicBool) so a late, off-lock store after shutdown is suppressed (watcher never resurrected post-shutdown).
+- `stop_file_watching` sets `file_watch_stopped`, aborts the in-flight startup task, then briefly locks to `stop_watching()` (teardown still detached).

 ## Acceptance Criteria
 - [x] `FileWatcher::stop_watching` and `EntityWatcher::drop` no longer block the caller on the notify-watcher teardown; the watcher object is dropped on a detached thread.
-- [ ] Measured: an in-process MCP server `shutdown()` drops from ~5s to milliseconds (record before/after for one server). -- SEE FINDINGS: teardown is now 60us, but shutdown is STILL ~4.5-5s due to a SECOND, distinct cause (lock contention with background startup). Teardown detach alone does NOT fix shutdown.
-- [ ] The previously-flaky tests pass FAST under full-workspace load (not just isolation): the in-process MCP-server cluster (`test_mcp_server_*`, `test_client_list_tools`/`test_client_call_tool`, kanban-app `state::tests` open-board), `mcp::file_watcher::tests::test_file_watcher_start_watching_replaces_previous`, and `swissarmyhammer-entity watcher::tests` (`entity_watcher_drop_sends_shutdown`, `test_attachment_create_emits_event`, `test_attachment_remove_emits_event`). If the event-delivery tests (attachment create/remove) remain slow because of FSEvents *event delivery* latency (a different facet than teardown), document that and file a follow-up rather than forcing it here. -- NOT MET: cluster still slow (18-45s each); see findings.
-- [ ] No change to observable watch behavior: prompt hot-reload and entity-cache event emission still work (existing event tests pass in isolation). -- met: all watcher event tests pass.
-- [ ] `cargo build --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check` clean.
+- [x] Measured: an in-process MCP server `shutdown()` drops from ~5s to milliseconds (record before/after for one server). -- DONE: `server.shutdown()` measured at **21us** (was ~5057ms). See profiling below. The lock-contention root cause is fixed.
+- [ ] The previously-flaky in-process MCP-server cluster passes FAST under full-workspace load. -- NOT MET BY THIS FIX: a THIRD, distinct facet remains — FSEvents stream **REGISTRATION** (`.watch()`, ~4.5s) on the background startup task pins a tokio worker and blocks runtime/test teardown. Filed as follow-up 01KS68DBMDTERYQSEP04JPJR2Z. Per the approved "STOP and report if it still doesn't pass under load — do not re-add workarounds" instruction, this is reported, not forced.
+- [x] No change to observable watch behavior: prompt hot-reload and entity-cache event emission still work (existing event tests pass).
+- [x] `cargo build --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check` clean. (Verified for swissarmyhammer-tools + swissarmyhammer-entity; fmt --check clean workspace-wide.)
 - [x] No serial guards or nextest `slow-timeout` overrides reintroduced.

 ## Tests
-- [x] Unit/behavior test that `FileWatcher::stop_watching` returns promptly (e.g. completes in well under 1s even after watching a real dir) — guards against a regression that re-blocks teardown. (`test_stop_watching_returns_promptly`)
-- [x] Equivalent promptness guard for `EntityWatcher` drop. (`entity_watcher_drop_returns_promptly`)
-- [x] Existing watcher event tests still pass in isolation (no behavior regression).
-- [ ] Full-workspace reliability: run `cargo nextest run --workspace` up to 3 consecutive times (early-bail on any cluster timeout), confirming the FSEvents cluster passes fast. Record honest timings. -- BLOCKED on the second cause below.
-
-## Workflow
-- Use `/tdd` — add the teardown-promptness guard test(s) first, then detach the drops. Measure before/after server shutdown. Verify with consecutive full-workspace runs. #test-failure
+- [x] Unit/behavior test that `FileWatcher::stop_watching` returns promptly (`test_stop_watching_returns_promptly`).
+- [x] Equivalent promptness guard for `EntityWatcher` drop (`entity_watcher_drop_returns_promptly`).
+- [x] Off-lock-build guard `test_start_builds_without_shared_lock`: `FileWatcher::start` builds while the shared lock is held (proves it never needs the lock — would deadlock if it did).
+- [x] Shutdown-promptness guard `test_stop_file_watching_returns_promptly_during_inflight_registration`: `stop_file_watching()` returns in <1s (measured **0.02s**) while a registration is in flight (handle registered + aborted, stopped-flag suppression).
+- [x] Late-store suppression guard `test_stop_file_watching_suppresses_late_store`: a startup completing after shutdown does NOT resurrect an active watcher.

 ## IMPLEMENTATION FINDINGS (2026-05-21, claude-code) — STOPPED FOR GUIDANCE

-The teardown detach was implemented exactly as specified and is verified correct, BUT it does NOT fix the in-process server shutdown slowness. Profiling found a SECOND, distinct cause.
+The teardown detach AND the lock-contention fix (option 1 + non-blocking shutdown) are both DONE and verified. Together they fully fix in-process server `shutdown()`: **~5057ms -> 21us**.

-### Profiling data (single `test_client_list_tools`, after the teardown fix)
+### Profiling data (single `test_client_list_tools`, after BOTH fixes; probes since removed)
 ```
-TIMING start_mcp_server   = 22.5 ms
-TIMING   lock acquire     = 4.95 s   <-- the real cost
-TIMING   stop_watching    = 60 us    <-- teardown detach works perfectly
-TIMING stop_file_watching = 4.95 s
-TIMING server.shutdown    = 4.95 s
+PROBE start_mcp_server   = 19.1 ms
+PROBE create_test_client = 7.2  ms   (handshake)
+PROBE list_tools         = 9.7  ms
+PROBE client.cancel      = 0.5  ms
+PROBE server.shutdown    = 21   us   <-- FIXED (was ~5057ms)
+PROBE FileWatcher::start = 4.55 s    <-- THIRD facet (registration), not shutdown
+PASS  test wall time     = 4.59 s
 ```

-### Diagnosis
-- The teardown detach is correct: `FileWatcher::stop_watching` now drops the `AsyncDebouncer` in 60us (was the suspected ~5s). Detach works.
-- The actual ~5s cost in `shutdown()` is the `self.file_watcher.lock().await` in `McpServer::stop_file_watching` (server.rs:1121). It waits ~4.95s for the tokio Mutex.
-- The lock is held by the BACKGROUND `start_file_watching` task (spawned by `spawn_background_file_watcher`), which holds `file_watcher.lock()` across the entire `FileWatcher::start_watching()` — and on macOS the slow op there is registering the FSEvents stream: `debouncer.watcher().watch(path, RecursiveMode::Recursive)` (NOT the drop). So shutdown blocks behind startup finishing its watch registration, then tears it down.
-- This is a different FSEvents facet (stream CREATION/REGISTRATION under a held lock) than the task's stated root cause (stream TEARDOWN). The task explicitly anticipated this kind of split: "If the teardown fix does not make the cluster reliably pass under load, STOP and report with data rather than re-adding workarounds."
-
-### What is DONE and verified
-- Teardown detach in both sites (`FileWatcher::stop_watching`, `EntityWatcher::Drop`) with WHY doc comments. `std::thread::spawn`, not spawn_blocking.
-- `EntityWatcher._watcher` -> `Option<RecommendedWatcher>` so Drop can move it out.
-- Two promptness guard tests added (TDD: EntityWatcher test went RED at 1.16s pre-fix, GREEN post-fix; FileWatcher drop now 60us).
-- All `swissarmyhammer-entity watcher::tests` and `swissarmyhammer-tools mcp::file_watcher::tests` pass (102 tests). In-process server cluster tests PASS but are still SLOW (~5s shutdown each).
+### Why the cluster is still slow (THIRD, distinct facet)
+- All measured phases sum to ~36ms; the ~4.5s test tail is NOT in any awaited code.
+- It is the synchronous FSEvents `.watch()` REGISTRATION (~4.5s) running on a background `tokio::spawn` startup task. `shutdown()` aborts that task, but `abort()` cannot interrupt a synchronous in-flight `.watch()`, so the tokio worker stays pinned until `.watch()` returns; the `#[tokio::test(multi_thread)]` runtime drop then waits for that worker.
+- This is distinct from facet (1) teardown drop [fixed] and facet (2) shutdown lock-contention [fixed]. Fixing it requires running `.watch()` off a tokio worker (e.g. dedicated detached std::thread delivering the debouncer back via a channel) — an architectural change beyond the approved "fix shutdown now" scope. Filed as 01KS68DBMDTERYQSEP04JPJR2Z.

-### Decision needed (would extend beyond the assigned plan)
-Options to actually make shutdown fast (pick one before I proceed):
-1. Make `start_file_watching` register the FSEvents watch WITHOUT holding `file_watcher.lock()` across the slow `.watch()` call (e.g. build+watch the debouncer first, then briefly lock only to store it; on shutdown, if startup is mid-flight, signal it to abort and skip storing). 
-2. On shutdown, `try_lock()` the watcher and, if the startup task still holds it, abort that background task (via its JoinHandle) instead of awaiting the lock — let the half-built debouncer drop on its own detached thread.
-3. Detach the FSEvents `.watch()` registration off the lock-held critical path during startup.
-
-I have NOT implemented any of these — they go beyond the assigned teardown-detach plan. Awaiting guidance.
\ No newline at end of file
+### Decision: STOP and report (per approved instruction)
+The assigned fix is complete and verified with data. `shutdown()` is fixed (5s -> 21us). The remaining cluster slowness is a separate, newly-isolated facet (FSEvents registration), reported as a follow-up rather than forced with a workaround, exactly as instructed. Task left in `doing` pending guidance on whether to pursue the registration facet now.
\ No newline at end of file
